label: Feature Engineering
definition: The craft of turning raw data into informative inputs, scaling, encoding, combining, extracting, so a model can learn from it. For decades it was the highest-leverage step in machine learning, often mattering more than the choice of algorithm; deep learning then automated most of it for perceptual data, the clearest single illustration of the bitter lesson.

body.text: Feature engineering is the work of turning raw data into something a model can actually learn from. Raw records almost never arrive in the form a learner handles best: numbers need scaling so no single feature dominates, categories need encoding into numbers, dates need decomposing into useful parts like day-of-week, missing values need handling, and domain knowledge often suggests combining or deriving signals that expose the structure of the problem. A good feature makes the relationship the model has to learn simpler and more visible; a bad representation can hide a pattern so thoroughly that no algorithm finds it.

For most of machine learning's history this was the highest-leverage activity in the entire pipeline, and the quiet truth of the field was that features mattered more than models. With a linear regression, a logistic regression, or a gradient-boosted tree, careful features frequently decided the outcome more than the choice of algorithm did, and the adage "garbage in, garbage out" was really a statement about representation: a model can only be as good as the features it is fed. A practitioner's real skill was often skill at inventing features.

Deep learning changed this balance, and the change is the cleanest example of the bitter lesson in action. A neural network learns its own internal features, its representations, directly from raw data, which is precisely why it transformed vision and language: it automated, and then surpassed, the feature engineering humans used to do by hand, and the learned embedding became the modern, automatic counterpart of an engineered feature. The decades of hand-built edge detectors and phonetic features were not refined but replaced, by features the network discovered for itself.

The craft did not vanish, though, it retreated to where learning has not yet won. On tabular and structured data, hand-built features still routinely beat what a network learns, which is part of why gradient-boosted trees remain dominant there, and even with deep models the upstream work of cleaning, normalizing, and structuring data remains essential. What changed is the boundary: for perceptual data, representation learning now does the job feature engineering once required, while for the spreadsheet-shaped problems that make up much of real-world machine learning, the human craft of building good features is still very much alive.
